text: You met great contacts at the conference. Now you have to lead the meeting.
How do I keep a discussion on track without being rude?

Thanks. The sample is too small, so we need more patients. By the way, the new coffee machine is broken again.
That is a fair point, but let us come back to that later. Thank you for the contribution on the sample, David.
Of course. So, who recruits the extra patients?
Good question. Let us allocate that action point to you, David, with a deadline of two weeks.
Let us wrap up. To sum up, we have three action points, and I will take the minutes. Thank you, everyone.

Welcome to this short session on how to lead a research discussion in English. Chairing a meeting is a skill, and a few simple phrases make a big difference. First, open clearly. State the objective of the meeting in one sentence, so everyone knows why they are there. A simple line works well: the objective today is to agree on the next steps for the study. Second, invite contributions. A good facilitator does not do all the talking. Ask each person directly: Maria, what is your view on the sample size? This brings in the quieter voices and gives you a richer discussion. Third, keep the group on track. If someone goes off topic, redirect them politely: that is an interesting point, but let us come back to it later. Fourth, manage the work. As decisions are made, allocate each action point to a specific person, with a clear deadline. Finally, wrap up. Before everyone leaves, summarize the main decisions, repeat who is responsible for what, and confirm that someone is taking the minutes. A good chair turns a long meeting into a short list of clear action points.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the clinical team. I am calling about the research discussion you are chairing tomorrow morning. I just wanted to give you a quick heads-up before you facilitate the meeting. The main objective, as I understand it, is to decide how we move the glycemic control study forward. Two things from my side. First, I would like to make a contribution on the recruitment problem, so please give me a few minutes on the agenda for that. Second, I think we should allocate a clear owner to each action point this time, because last month several tasks had no name next to them and nothing happened. If you can summarize the decisions at the end and make sure someone takes the minutes, that would really help all of us. Thank you for leading this, Patricia. I know the discussion will be much more productive with you in the chair. See you tomorrow, and feel free to call me back if you want to talk it through before the meeting.

First, open the meeting and state the objective in one clear sentence. Next, invite each person to make a contribution, and bring in the quieter voices. After that, if someone goes off topic, redirect the discussion politely. Then, as decisions are made, allocate each action point to a specific person. Finally, wrap up: summarize the decisions and make sure someone takes the minutes.
